title: Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age
caption:
Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age, 2022
Percentage of children at lower secondary school age meeting reading comprehension standards. This data accounts for school completion rates to estimate reading skills for all children, not just those in school.

This bar chart ranks countries by the percentage of children at lower secondary school age who meet reading comprehension standards, with the data adjusted to account for school completion rates to estimate skills for all children, not just those currently in school. The vertical axis measures the share of proficient readers from 0% to 80%, while the horizontal axis lists individual nations ordered from highest to lowest performance. Bars are color-coded by income group, distinguishing low-income, lower-middle-income, upper-middle-income, and high-income countries. Ireland leads the distribution with a share exceeding 80%, followed closely by Australia and Lithuania, all categorized as high-income nations where the bars reach the top of the scale. Spain and Turkey also appear near the upper end, with Turkey falling into the upper-middle-income band. Moving down the ranking, Greece and Moldova show shares between 60% and 70%, while Brazil and Kazakhstan sit in the 40% to 50% range. The lower portion of the chart features Azerbaijan, the Dominican Republic, and Cambodia, where the bars drop below 20%. Cambodia records the lowest visible value, falling well under the 10% mark. High-income countries dominate the left side of the chart with dark green bars, while lighter green and pink bars representing upper-middle and lower-middle income groups cluster toward the right. The visual arrangement shows a steep decline in proficiency shares as one moves from the highest-ranking nations to the lowest, with the gap between the top and bottom performers spanning more than 70 percentage points.
